name: Groupe ADP (Aéroports de Paris)
notes: French state holds 50.6%; Vinci SA 8%; Dutch state (via Schiphol Group) 8%; free float ~33.4%. Listed on Euronext Paris. Owns 46.1% of TAV Airports Holding.

name: VINCI Airports (incl. ANA Aeroportos de Portugal)
notes: 100% subsidiary of VINCI SA (private). Operates 10 airports in Portugal via ANA Aeroportos de Portugal (acquired 2013); 10 airports in France; BEG (Serbia, 25-year concession); BUD (Hungary, 20% stake as platform operator since Jun 2024, Hungarian state Corvinus 80%); BFS (Northern Ireland); LGW (UK, majority stake); NYO (Sweden, Skavsta). ~154 million pax across European network.

name: Aena SME SA
notes: ENAIRE (Spanish state) holds 51%; 49% free-float on Bolsa Madrid. World's largest airport group by passenger volume. Manages 46 airports and 2 heliports in Spain. International: LTN (London Luton, 51%); LBA (Leeds Bradford, 51% of controlling holdco, Dec 2025); NCL (Newcastle, holdco holds 49% of airport, Dec 2025). ~369m pax globally in 2024.

name: Fraport AG Frankfurt Airport Services Worldwide
notes: State of Hesse 31.3%, City of Frankfurt (via Stadtwerke) 20.9%, Deutsche Lufthansa AG 8.4%, free float ~39.4%. Listed on Frankfurt Stock Exchange (MDAX). Parent of Fraport Greece (73.4%), Fraport Twin Star Bulgaria (60%), Fraport Slovenija (75.5%), and 50% JV for Antalya (AYT) with TAV. Directly operates Frankfurt Airport.

name: Fraport Regional Airports of Greece Management Company SA
notes: 73.4% Fraport AG; 26.6% Copelouzos Group (Greece). 40-year concession (2016–2055) from Greek state covering 14 regional airports. Concession value approx. €1.23 billion upfront.

name: Fraport Twin Star Airport Management AD
notes: 60% Fraport AG; 40% Airport Services Bulgaria. 35-year concession (2006–2041) from Bulgarian state covering the two Black Sea coast airports.

name: Fraport Slovenija d.o.o.
notes: 75.5% Fraport AG; remainder Slovenian state-linked entities. Acquired through 2014 privatisation. Sole international airport in Slovenia.

name: Royal Schiphol Group NV
notes: Dutch Ministry of Finance 69.77%; Municipality of Amsterdam 20.03%; Municipality of Rotterdam 2.2%; Groupe ADP 8.0%. 100% of AMS, RTM, LEY; 51% of EIN (Eindhoven); 40% of MST (Maastricht Aachen). Lelystad (LEY) is cargo/GA-focused.

name: TAV Havalimanları Holding AŞ
notes: Groupe ADP 46.1%; free float ~47.6% on Borsa Istanbul; other ~6.3%. Listed on Istanbul exchange. Operates in Turkey, Georgia, North Macedonia and Croatia. Antalya (AYT) via 50/50 JV with Fraport. 107 million pax in 2024.

name: iGA Havalimanı İşletmesi AŞ (Istanbul Grand Airport)
notes: Kalyon Havacılık 55%; Cengiz İnşaat 45%. 25-year BOT concession from Turkish government (from 2018 opening). Concession value €26.1bn. IST is Europe's busiest and world's 3rd-busiest international airport.

name: İstanbul Sabiha Gökcen Uluslararası Havalimanı İşletmesi AŞ
notes: Sabiha Gökcen operated under BOT concession; Malaysia Airports Holdings Berhad (MAHB) holds a majority stake in the operating company (ISG) with Turkish partners. Concession expires 2032.

name: Devlet Hava Meydanları İşletmesi Genel Müdürlüğü
notes: 100% Turkish state (Ministry of Transport). Manages all Turkish airports not under private concession. List is representative of major passenger airports; total network is larger.

name: Flughafen München GmbH
notes: Free State of Bavaria 51%; Federal Republic of Germany 26%; City of Munich 23%. Fully publicly owned. Germany's second-busiest airport.

name: Flughafen Berlin Brandenburg GmbH
notes: State of Berlin 37%; State of Brandenburg 37%; Federal Republic of Germany 26%. Opened October 2020, consolidating TXL and SXF traffic. Fully publicly owned.

name: Flughafen Hamburg GmbH
notes: City of Hamburg (Free and Hanseatic City) 51%; AviAlliance GmbH (PSP Investments, Canada) 49%. Germany's fifth-busiest airport.

name: Flughafen Düsseldorf GmbH
notes: City of Düsseldorf 50%; AviAlliance (PSP Investments) 30%; daa International (Ireland) 20%. Germany's third-busiest airport.

name: Aeroporti di Roma SpA
notes: Mundys SpA 99.39% (Edizione/Benetton ~57%, Blackstone ~37.8%, Fondazione CRT ~5%); local authorities per DPCM decree ~0.61%. Mundys is the renamed Atlantia post-2022 takeover. FCO is Europe's 4th-busiest airport.

name: SEA Aeroporti di Milano SpA
notes: Municipality of Milan ~84.56%; F2i SGR ~9.89%; other ~5.55%. Directly operates MXP (Malpensa) and LIN (Linate). Holds 31% minority stake in SACBO (operator of BGY, Bergamo).

name: SACBO SpA
notes: Bergamo Province ~28%, Bergamo Chamber of Commerce ~21%, Bergamo Municipality ~20%, SEA SpA 31%. Consortium of local public and private entities. Operates Milan Bergamo, Italy's third-busiest airport.

name: Gesac SpA
notes: 2i Aeroporti SpA (F2i Third Fund 51% + Asterion Industrial Partners 49%) holds 87%; local authorities (Campania Region, Naples municipality) ~13%. F2i has held majority since 2010; Asterion acquired 49% of 2i Aeroporti in 2024. Operates Naples International Airport.

name: Save SpA (Aeroporto di Venezia)
notes: Venice Municipality and related public entities ~55%; institutional investors ~45%. Listed on Borsa Italiana. Operates Venice Marco Polo and Treviso Antonio Canova airports.

name: Toscana Aeroporti SpA
notes: Corporación América Italia SpA (CAAP 100%) 62.28%; Toscana Aeroporti Investimenti (local public entities incl. Tuscany Region, Florence/Pisa municipalities) ~29%; free float ~8.7%. Listed on Borsa Italiana (TYA.MI). Formed 2015 merger of SAT (Pisa) and AdF (Florence). CAAP increased stake to 100% of CAI in 2025.

name: Aeroporti di Puglia SpA
notes: Puglia Regional Government ~99%. Operates four commercial airports across the Apulia region. FOG (Foggia) and TAR (Grottaglie) have limited scheduled service.

name: Aéroports de la Côte d'Azur SA
notes: Azzurra Aeroporti (Mundys 52.51%, EDF Invest 24.99%, Principality of Monaco 12.5%, ADR 10%) holds 64%; CCI Nice Côte d'Azur 25%; Région PACA and other public bodies ~11%. NCE is France's 2nd-busiest airport. CEQ (Cannes Mandelieu) and LTT (La Môle Saint-Tropez) are seasonal/charter focused.

name: Manchester Airports Group plc
notes: Manchester City Council 35.5%; nine other Greater Manchester local authorities 29%; IFM Global Infrastructure Fund (Australia) 35.5%. Operates UK's 2nd, 4th, and 8th busiest airports.

name: Heathrow Airport Holdings Limited
notes: Ardian (France) 32.61%; Qatar Investment Authority 20%; Saudi PIF 15.01%; GIC (Singapore) 11.2%; Australian Retirement Trust 11.18%; China Investment Corporation 10%. PIF and Ardian acquired their stakes from Ferrovial in Dec 2024. Operates only LHR following divestment of all other BAA airports post-2012. LHR is Europe's busiest airport.

name: Edinburgh Airport Limited
notes: Global Infrastructure Partners (GIP, now BlackRock) 100%. Acquired from BAA in 2012. Scotland's busiest airport, handling ~12 million pax in 2024.

name: AGS Airports Limited
notes: AviAlliance (PSP Investments, Canada) 78%; Blackstone 22%. AviAlliance completed £1.53bn acquisition from Ferrovial/Macquarie in Jan 2024, then sold 22% stake to Blackstone. Operates Glasgow, Aberdeen and Southampton airports.

name: daa plc
notes: 100% Irish state (semi-state commercial entity). Formerly Dublin Airport Authority. Operates Dublin (Ireland's largest, 34.6m pax in 2024) and Cork airports. International arm daa International holds 20% stake in Düsseldorf Airport.

name: Copenhagen Airports A/S
notes: Danish state acquired ~98% in Dec 2024 deal (Ministry of Finance pre-existing ~38.75% + ATP pension fund purchase of 59.4%). State plans to divest to a ~51% majority long-term. Copenhagen is the Nordic countries' largest airport (~30m pax in 2024).

name: Billund Airport A/S
notes: Billund Municipality ~49%; KIRKBI AS (LEGO family) ~26%; Midtjylland Region ~25%. Denmark's second-largest airport, hub for South Jutland.

name: Société de l'Aéroport de Luxembourg SA (lux-Airport)
notes: 100% Luxembourg state owned. Operates the Grand Duchy's sole commercial airport.

name: Malta International Airport plc
notes: Malta Mediterranean Link Consortium (Flughafen Wien AG 96% stake) 40%; Government of Malta 20%; VIE Malta Limited (Flughafen Wien separately) 10.1%; free float on Malta Stock Exchange ~29.9%. Flughafen Wien AG effectively controls ~48.5% of MIA in total. Privatised 2002; sole commercial airport in Malta.

name: Avinor AS
notes: 100% Norwegian state (Ministry of Transport). Operates 43 commercial passenger airports and 1 heliport. Includes Oslo Gardermoen, the country's main hub, and an extensive network of regional/remote airports. 51.4 million pax in 2024.

name: Swedavia AB
notes: 100% Swedish state. Operates Sweden's ten busiest airports. Stockholm Arlanda (ARN) handles ~26m pax annually. BMA (Bromma) primarily serves domestic routes.

name: Finavia Oyj
notes: 100% Finnish state (Prime Minister's Office). Successor to the Civil Aviation Administration. Operates 20 airports total, 18 serving commercial flights. Helsinki-Vantaa (HEL) accounts for ~80% of network traffic. Note: ENF (Enontekiö) has no scheduled service; serves seasonal winter charter flights only.

name: Flughafen Zürich AG
notes: Canton of Zurich 33.33% + 1 share; City of Zurich 5%; free float ~61.67% on SIX Swiss Exchange (ticker: FHZN). Switzerland's largest airport and main hub. International arm Flughafen Zürich International operates concessions in Brazil, Chile, Colombia and Curaçao.

name: Aéroport International de Genève (Genève Aéroport)
notes: 100% Canton of Geneva. Operates under a special public law statute. Unique bilateral status with France; some facilities are on French territory. Switzerland's second-largest airport.

name: Isavia ohf.
notes: 100% Icelandic state. Formed 2010 from merger of Keflavik International Airport and Flugstodir. Operates 12 scheduled passenger airports and ~30 landing strips. KEF handles ~9m pax annually.

name: Przedsiębiorstwo Państwowe Porty Lotnicze
notes: 100% Polish state (Ministry of Infrastructure). Directly operates Warsaw Chopin (WAW). Holds majority/minority stakes in other Polish airports: KRK (76.2%), RZE (48%), POZ (39%), SZZ (36.9%), GDN (26.1%), WMI/Modlin (27.4%), WRO and BZG. WAW handled a record 21.3m pax in 2024.

name: Letiště Praha as
notes: 100% Czech state (Ministry of Finance). Operates Václav Havel Airport Prague (formerly Ruzyně). Sole major hub for the Czech Republic; ~16 million pax in 2024.

name: Flughafen Wien AG
notes: Airports Group Europe (IFM Investors, Australia) 38.16%; Province of Lower Austria 20%; City of Vienna 20%; Employee Foundation 10%; free float ~11.84%. Listed on Vienna Stock Exchange (Prime Market, ATX). Primarily operates VIE; also holds minority stake in Klagenfurt Airport (KLU).

name: Brussels Airport Company NV/SA
notes: Belgian federal government (SFPI/FPIM) 25%; BAISA consortium 75% (Macquarie ~39%, Ontario Teachers' Pension Plan ~30%, Japanese/Australian institutional investors remainder). Belgium's main international hub.

name: Brussels South Charleroi Airport SA (BSCA)
notes: Walloon Region of Belgium ~75%; SCA (SOWAER) ~25%. Low-cost hub, primary base for Ryanair in Belgium. Processes ~8 million pax annually.

name: Athens International Airport SA
notes: AviAlliance (PSP Investments) acquired majority, holding just over 50% following 2024 IPO of 30% stake; Greek state (Hellenic Republic Asset Development Fund) ~25%; remainder free float. Concession extended to 2046. Handles ~30m pax annually.

name: CN Aeroporturi București SA
notes: 100% Romanian state (Ministry of Transport). Operates both Bucharest-area airports. OTP (Henri Coandă) is Romania's main international gateway; BBU (Băneasa Aurel Vlaicu) serves limited charter/business traffic.

name: SOF Connect AD
notes: Meridiam (France) holds ~99% of SOF Connect AD; Munich Airport acts as operational/technical partner. 35-year concession from Bulgarian state, operational from April 2021. Includes €615m investment commitment. Sofia Airport (SOF) is Bulgaria's main international gateway, handling ~6.5m pax annually. Plovdiv Airport (PDV) is a separate state-owned entity (Plovdiv Airport EAD) not included here.

name: Riga International Airport (Starptautiskā lidosta Rīga)
notes: 100% Latvian state. Largest airport in the Baltic states, handling ~7.8 million pax in 2024.

name: Tallinna Lennujaam AS (Tallinn Airport)
notes: 100% Estonian state (via state-owned Tallinna Lennujaam AS). Operates all Estonian airports including Tallinn, Tartu, and Kuressaare.

name: UAB Oro Uostai (Lithuanian Airports)
notes: 100% Lithuanian state (Ministry of Transport). Operates three commercial passenger airports under one management entity.

name: Edeis
notes: Private French airport management company (formerly SNC-Lavalin Airport Solutions, rebranded 2017). Manages 20+ regional French airports under concession agreements with local authorities. Largest regional airport operator in France by number of airports. Also operates airports in Réunion and Mayotte.

name: Chambre de Commerce et d'Industrie de la Corse
notes: Corsican airports operated by local CCIs under concessions from the Collectivité de Corse. CCI de Corse-du-Sud manages Ajaccio and Figari; CCI de Haute-Corse manages Bastia and Calvi. Public bodies funded by regional taxes and airport fees.

name: Aéroport Marseille Provence SA
notes: CCI Aix-Marseille-Provence ~25%; Région Sud-PACA ~6.5%; Département des Bouches-du-Rhône ~4.5%; other local public entities ~4%. Private shareholders: Schiphol Group and SAS Aéro (consortium) hold ~60%. France's 3rd-busiest airport after CDG and ORY. ~10M pax.

name: SAGEB (Société Aéroportuaire de Gestion et d'Exploitation de Beauvais)
notes: Operates Beauvais-Tillé under concession from the Syndicat Mixte de l'Aéroport de Beauvais-Tillé (SMABT). Transdev (Caisse des Dépôts/RATP Dev group) holds the operating concession. Major Ryanair base; France's 10th-busiest airport by passenger volume.

name: Société Aéroportuaire de Lille-Lesquin SA
notes: Syndicat Mixte des Aérodromes de Lille-Lesquin (local public authorities including Métropole Européenne de Lille, Région Hauts-de-France) ~60%; private partners ~40%. Operates Lille-Lesquin Airport under a mixed-economy model.

name: Aéroport Toulouse-Blagnac SA
notes: Eiffage Group (via CASIL Europe acquisition) 49.99%; local public authorities (Région Occitanie, Toulouse Métropole, Département Haute-Garonne, CCI Toulouse) ~40%; French state (via DGAC) ~10.01%. Eiffage acquired the private stake from Chinese consortium CASIL in 2022. France's 4th-busiest airport.

name: Flughafen Bremen GmbH
notes: City of Bremen (Stadtgemeinde Bremen) 100%. Fully municipally owned. Serves the Bremen/Lower Saxony region.

name: Flughafen Köln/Bonn GmbH
notes: Federal Republic of Germany 30.94%; State of North Rhine-Westphalia 30.94%; City of Cologne 31.12%; City of Bonn 6.06%; Rhein-Sieg-Kreis 0.59%; Rheinisch-Bergischer Kreis 0.35%. Fully publicly owned. Major cargo hub (UPS European hub) and passenger airport.

name: Flughafen Dortmund GmbH
notes: City of Dortmund 74%; Dortmunder Stadtwerke AG (city utility company) 26%. Fully publicly owned. Smaller regional airport serving the eastern Ruhr area.

name: Regia Autonomă Aeroportul Internațional Avram Iancu Cluj
notes: 100% Cluj County Council (autonomous regional authority). Romania's second-busiest airport after Bucharest Henri Coandă. Serves Transylvania and northwest Romania.

name: Flughafen Stuttgart GmbH
notes: State of Baden-Württemberg 65%; City of Stuttgart 35%. Fully publicly owned. Germany's sixth-busiest airport, primary hub for Baden-Württemberg.

name: Flughafen Hannover-Langenhagen GmbH
notes: State of Lower Saxony 35%; City of Hannover 30%; Fraport AG 30%; Hannoversche Beteiligungsgesellschaft 5%. Mixed public-private ownership. Major trade fair gateway (Hannover Messe).

name: Flughafen Nürnberg GmbH
notes: City of Nuremberg 50%; Free State of Bavaria 50%. Fully publicly owned. Second-largest airport in Bavaria after Munich.

name: Mitteldeutsche Flughafen AG
notes: Free State of Saxony 77.84%; City of Dresden 6.14%; City of Leipzig 2.1%; City of Halle 2.1%; City of Schkeuditz 2.05%; others ~9.77%. Holds 94% of Flughafen Leipzig/Halle GmbH and 94% of Flughafen Dresden GmbH. LEJ is Europe's 5th-largest cargo airport (DHL European hub).

name: Allgäu Airport GmbH & Co. KG
notes: Majority privately held; public shareholders include Unterallgäu district, City of Memmingen, and surrounding municipalities (~26%). Low-cost airport converted from former military airbase in 2004. Ryanair and Wizz Air base.

name: Airport Weeze Flughafen Niederrhein GmbH
notes: Privately owned by Dutch entrepreneur consortium (Airport Network BV). Former RAF/NATO airbase converted to civilian airport in 2003. Low-cost carrier focused, primarily Ryanair. Located near the Dutch border.

name: Flughafen Paderborn/Lippstadt GmbH
notes: Owned by districts of Paderborn and Höxter, cities of Paderborn, Bielefeld, and surrounding municipalities. Fully publicly owned regional airport serving Ostwestfalen-Lippe.

name: Baden Airpark GmbH
notes: State of Baden-Württemberg ~51.4%; Landkreis Rastatt ~24.9%; City of Baden-Baden ~6.6%; others ~17.1%. Converted from former Canadian Forces Base Söllingen. Combined airport and business park.

name: Flughafen Saarbrücken GmbH
notes: Saarland state ~51%; Landeshauptstadt Saarbrücken (city) ~49%. Fully publicly owned. Smallest German airport with scheduled service.

name: Triwo Hahn Airport GmbH
notes: Triwo AG (German real estate group) acquired the airport out of insolvency in 2023. Former US military base (Hahn Air Base). Historically a major Ryanair hub marketed as 'Frankfurt-Hahn' despite being 120km from Frankfurt. Cargo and low-cost passenger operations.

name: Groningen Airport Eelde NV
notes: Province of Drenthe 30%; Province of Groningen 26%; Municipality of Tynaarlo 24%; Municipality of Groningen 20%. Fully publicly owned. Northern Netherlands' only commercial airport. Limited scheduled service with seasonal holiday charter flights.

name: Birmingham Airport Holdings Limited
notes: Seven West Midlands metropolitan borough councils jointly hold 51% (Birmingham City Council is the largest single shareholder); Ontario Teachers' Pension Plan holds 49%. UK's seventh-busiest airport.

name: Bristol Airport Limited
notes: Ontario Teachers' Pension Plan Board 100% (acquired from Macquarie in 2014). Southwest England's largest airport. Serves Bristol, Bath, and South Wales.

name: Peel Airports Limited
notes: 100% subsidiary of Peel Group (Peel Holdings). Operates Liverpool John Lennon Airport and Robin Hood Doncaster Sheffield (currently closed for redevelopment). Peel Group is a private UK infrastructure and real estate conglomerate.

name: Regional & City Airports Limited (Rigby Group)
notes: 100% owned by Rigby Group (private UK family-owned conglomerate). Operates Exeter, Coventry, and Norwich airports. Also operates Bournemouth and City of Derry airports under management contracts.

name: Belfast City Airport Finance Ltd
notes: 3i Infrastructure plc acquired the airport in 2024. George Best Belfast City Airport, a city centre airport with a short runway. Competes with Belfast International (BFS, operated by VINCI).

name: Welsh Government (Cardiff Airport)
notes: Welsh Government 100%. Nationalised in 2013 (purchased from TBI/Abertis for £52m). Wales' only international airport. Operated through holding company Cardiff International Airport Ltd.

name: Highlands and Islands Airports Limited (HIAL)
notes: 100% Scottish Government (via Transport Scotland). Operates 11 airports across the Scottish Highlands and Islands. Most are lifeline services for remote communities. Dundee Airport is the group's largest mainland airport.

name: Glasgow Prestwick Airport Limited
notes: Scottish Government 100%. Nationalised in 2013 to prevent closure. Formerly a major transatlantic hub and Ryanair base. Now primarily cargo (Amazon Air hub) with limited scheduled passenger service.

name: London City Airport Ltd
notes: GIP (Global Infrastructure Partners, now BlackRock) and consortium including ADIA (Abu Dhabi), AIMCo (Alberta), and Wren House (Kuwait). Acquired from Albert Balasingam for £2bn in 2016. STOL operations on a short runway in London Docklands. Primarily business travel focused.

name: London Southend Airport Company Limited
notes: Esken Limited (formerly Stobart Group) owns the airport. Currently has very limited scheduled service following easyJet's withdrawal. Undergoing strategic review.

name: Bournemouth Airport Limited
notes: Regional & City Airports (Rigby Group) operates under management contract. Manchester Airports Group sold the airport to Rigby Group in 2017. Seasonal leisure focus, primarily TUI and Ryanair.

name: Jersey Airport / Ports of Jersey
notes: 100% States of Jersey (Jersey Government). Ports of Jersey is a wholly government-owned entity operating both the airport and harbour. Jersey is a Crown Dependency, not part of the UK.

name: Guernsey Airport / States of Guernsey
notes: 100% States of Guernsey. Government-operated airport. Guernsey is a Crown Dependency, not part of the UK.

name: Isle of Man Airport / Isle of Man Government
notes: 100% Isle of Man Government (Department of Infrastructure). Crown Dependency. Ronaldsway Airport is the island's sole commercial airport.

name: Shannon Group plc
notes: 100% Irish state. Separated from daa (Dublin Airport Authority) in 2014 to operate independently. Operates Shannon Airport and Shannon Free Zone (the world's first free trade zone, established 1959). Ireland's third-busiest airport.

name: Ireland West Airport Knock (IWAK)
notes: Community-owned not-for-profit company with public funding from Irish government, local authorities, and EU. Founded in 1985 by Monsignor James Horan. Serves the West of Ireland (Mayo, Sligo, Galway, Roscommon).

name: Kerry Airport plc
notes: Kerry County Council ~34%; private shareholders ~66%. Publicly traded on the Irish Stock Exchange (Enterprise Securities Market). Small regional airport serving County Kerry and the southwest.

name: Donegal Airport (Carrickfinn)
notes: Donegal County Council. Small regional airport serving the northwest, with PSO (Public Service Obligation) route to Dublin operated by Emerald Airlines.

name: Waterford Airport plc
notes: Waterford City and County Council ~51%; local private investors ~49%. Currently has very limited scheduled service. Primarily GA and flight training.

name: Aalborg Lufthavn A/S
notes: Aalborg Municipality 100%. Denmark's third-busiest airport. Serves North Jutland. Significant military co-use (Aalborg Air Base, Royal Danish Air Force).

name: Aarhus Airport A/S
notes: Owned by consortium of local municipalities (Syddjurs Municipality majority). Located at Tirstrup, ~40km from Aarhus city centre. Limited scheduled service to Copenhagen and a few European destinations.

name: Bornholms Lufthavn A/S
notes: Bornholm Regional Municipality 100%. Lifeline airport for the island of Bornholm in the Baltic Sea. PSO route to Copenhagen operated by DAT (Danish Air Transport).

name: Esbjerg Airport (Esbjerg Kommune)
notes: Esbjerg Municipality 100%. Primarily serves North Sea oil and gas helicopter traffic. Limited scheduled passenger service. Gateway to Fanø island.

name: Sønderborg Lufthavn (Sønderborg Kommune)
notes: Sønderborg Municipality 100%. Small regional airport serving southern Jutland. PSO route to Copenhagen. Near the German border.

name: Midtjyllands Lufthavn (Karup)
notes: Danish Defence (Forsvarsministeriet) owns the base; civilian terminal operated by a municipal consortium (Viborg, Herning, Silkeborg, Skive municipalities). Military airbase with co-located civilian operations.

name: Vagar Airport (Vága Floghavn)
notes: Faroese Government (Landsverk) 100%. Sole airport in the Faroe Islands (autonomous territory of Denmark). Operated by Vága Floghavn. Atlantic Airways is the primary carrier. Spectacular approach through mountain terrain.

name: Västerås Flygplats AB
notes: City of Västerås 100%. Alternative Stockholm airport ~100km west of the capital. Limited scheduled service. Primarily GA and flight training.

name: Örebro Airport AB
notes: Örebro Municipality 100%. Primarily charter and cargo. Limited scheduled service. Also used for military exercises.

name: Norrköping Flygplats (Norrköping kommun)
notes: Norrköping Municipality 100%. Kungsängen Airport. Small regional airport with limited scheduled service.

name: Ängelholm-Helsingborg Airport AB
notes: Ängelholm Municipality ~majority; Region Skåne minority. Former military airbase (F10 wing). Serves northwest Skåne. SAS operates scheduled service to Stockholm.

name: Kalmar Öland Airport AB
notes: Kalmar Municipality majority; Region Kalmar minority. Serves Kalmar and the island of Öland. Scheduled service to Stockholm Arlanda.

name: Växjö Småland Airport AB
notes: Växjö Municipality majority. Small regional airport serving Småland. Limited scheduled service.

name: Halmstad City Airport (Halmstad kommun)
notes: Halmstad Municipality 100%. Former military airbase. Limited civilian scheduled service.

name: Sundsvall-Timrå Airport AB
notes: Sundsvall and Timrå municipalities jointly owned. Midnorrland Airport. Serves the Sundsvall/Härnösand region of central Sweden.

name: Hermes Airports Ltd
notes: Consortium: Bouygues Bâtiment International, VINCI Airports, Cyprus Trading Corporation, Iacovou Brothers, Hellenic Mining. 25-year BOT concession (2006–2031) from the Republic of Cyprus. Operates both Cypriot airports. Combined ~11M pax annually. VINCI Airports holds the largest stake.

name: Letisko M. R. Štefánika – Airport Bratislava (BTS)
notes: Slovak Republic (Ministry of Transport) 100%. State enterprise (štátny podnik). Slovakia's largest airport. Competes with nearby Vienna (VIE) for Bratislava passenger catchment. ~2.5M pax.

name: Letisko Košice – Airport Košice a.s.
notes: Slovak Republic (Ministry of Transport) ~66%; Košice Self-Governing Region ~34%. Slovakia's second-largest airport, serving eastern Slovakia. ~600K pax.

name: ANA Aeroportos de Portugal SA
notes: 100% VINCI Airports (acquired from the Portuguese state in 2013 for €3.08bn). Operates all 10 commercial airports in mainland Portugal, Azores, and Madeira under a 50-year concession (2013–2063). Lisbon (LIS) handles ~32M pax annually, making it the busiest airport on the Iberian Atlantic coast.

name: Budapest Airport Zrt
notes: Hungarian state (via Corvinus Nemzetközi Befektetési Zrt) 80%; VINCI Airports 20% (platform operator since Jun 2024). Renationalised in 2023 for €3.1bn after years of private ownership under AviAlliance. Hungary's sole major international airport. ~16M pax.

name: Međunarodna Zračna Luka Zagreb d.d. (MZLZ)
notes: ZAIC consortium (Groupe ADP 21%, TAV Airports 7.5%, local partners 71.5%) operates under a 30-year concession (2013–2043) from the Croatian state. New terminal opened 2017. Croatia's busiest airport, ~3.5M pax.

name: Zračna Luka Split d.o.o.
notes: Republic of Croatia 55%; Split-Dalmatia County 45%. Croatia's second-busiest airport and busiest on the Adriatic coast. Major seasonal tourist gateway. ~3.5M pax, heavily summer-weighted.

name: Zračna Luka Dubrovnik d.o.o.
notes: Republic of Croatia 55%; Dubrovnik-Neretva County 45%. Gateway to Dubrovnik and the southern Dalmatian coast. Heavily seasonal, ~2.5M pax. Expanded terminal 2017.

name: Zračna Luka Zadar d.o.o.
notes: Republic of Croatia 55%; Zadar County 45%. Major Ryanair base on the Adriatic. New terminal opened 2018. ~900K pax, growing rapidly.

name: Zračna Luka Rijeka d.o.o.
notes: Republic of Croatia 55%; Primorje-Gorski Kotar County 45%. Located on the island of Krk. Serves the Kvarner Gulf tourist region. ~200K pax, seasonal.

name: Zračna Luka Pula d.o.o.
notes: Republic of Croatia 55%; Istria County 45%. Gateway to the Istrian peninsula. Heavily seasonal charter traffic. ~700K pax.

name: Zračna Luka Osijek d.o.o.
notes: Republic of Croatia 55%; Osijek-Baranja County 45%. Small regional airport serving Slavonia (eastern Croatia). Very limited scheduled service.

name: Tirana International Airport SHPK (Nënë Tereza)
notes: Kastrati Group (Albania) acquired concession in 2024, taking over from China Everbright. 20-year BOT concession from the Albanian government. Albania's sole international airport. Rapid growth: ~4M pax, up from 2.5M pre-pandemic. New terminal under construction.

name: Airports of Montenegro JSC (Aerodromi Crne Gore AD)
notes: Government of Montenegro 100%. Operates both Montenegrin airports. TGD (Podgorica) is the capital city airport; TIV (Tivat) serves the Bay of Kotor tourist region. Combined ~2.5M pax, heavily seasonal at TIV.

name: Belgrade Nikola Tesla Airport (Aerodrom Nikola Tesla Beograd)
notes: VINCI Airports holds 100% operating concession (25-year concession from 2018). Serbian state retains ownership of the infrastructure. Serbia's main international gateway and Air Serbia hub. ~7M pax. Major expansion underway.

name: Aerodrom Niš Konstantin Veliki d.o.o.
notes: Republic of Serbia 100%. Serbia's second-largest airport. Low-cost carrier focus (Wizz Air base). Serves southern Serbia. ~500K pax.

name: Flughafen Graz Betriebsgesellschaft mbH
notes: Province of Styria 87%; City of Graz 13%. Fully publicly owned. Austria's second-busiest airport. Serves Styria and southern Austria. ~1M pax.

name: Innsbrucker Flughafen Betriebsgesellschaft mbH
notes: Province of Tyrol ~50%; City of Innsbruck ~50%. Fully publicly owned. Challenging mountain approach. Strong seasonal charter traffic for skiing. ~1M pax.

name: Salzburger Flughafen GmbH
notes: Province of Salzburg 50%; City of Salzburg 25%; private minority shareholders 25%. Austria's third-busiest airport. Gateway to the Austrian and Bavarian Alps. ~1.8M pax.

name: Flughafen Linz GesmbH (Blue Danube Airport)
notes: Province of Upper Austria ~64%; City of Linz ~27%; others ~9%. Fully publicly owned. Serves Upper Austria. Limited scheduled service. ~300K pax.

name: Kärntner Flughafen Betriebsgesellschaft mbH
notes: Province of Carinthia ~35%; City of Klagenfurt ~20%; Flughafen Wien AG holds minority stake via Lilihill. Small regional airport serving Carinthia. Limited scheduled service.

name: Letiště Brno a.s.
notes: South Moravian Region (Jihomoravský kraj) 100%. Czech Republic's second-largest airport. Serves Moravia. Low-cost and charter focus. ~500K pax.

name: Letiště Leoše Janáčka Ostrava a.s.
notes: Moravian-Silesian Region 100%. Czech Republic's third-largest airport. Serves the industrial region of Silesia and northern Moravia. ~300K pax.

name: Antwerp International Airport NV (Luchthaven Antwerpen)
notes: Flemish Region (via PMV/Vlaamse Overheid). City airport with short runway. Primarily business aviation and TUI fly Belgium. ~300K pax.

name: Internationale Luchthaven Oostende-Brugge NV
notes: Flemish Region (via PMV). Primarily charter and cargo. TUI fly Belgium seasonal base. Serves West Flanders. ~400K pax.

name: Liège Airport SA
notes: Walloon Region (via SOWAER/SAB) 100%. Europe's 7th-largest cargo airport. Major e-commerce hub (Alibaba/Cainiao European hub). Limited passenger service. ~300K pax but massive cargo volumes.

name: EuroAirport Basel-Mulhouse-Freiburg
notes: Unique binational airport on French soil, governed by a Franco-Swiss international treaty (1949). Joint Franco-Swiss public entity. Swiss and French customs sectors. Switzerland's third-largest airport. ~9M pax. IATA codes: BSL (Swiss side), MLH (French side), EAP (combined).

name: Flughafen Bern AG
notes: Canton of Bern and local municipalities ~50%; private shareholders ~50%. Small airport near the Swiss capital. Very limited scheduled service. Primarily GA and charter.

name: Aeroportul Internațional Traian Vuia Timișoara
notes: Timiș County Council 80%; Romanian Ministry of Transport 20%. Romania's third-busiest airport. Serves the Banat region and western Romania. Wizz Air base. ~1.5M pax.

name: Aeroportul Internațional Iași
notes: Iași County Council ~80%; Romanian Ministry of Transport ~20%. Serves Moldova region (eastern Romania). Rapid growth driven by diaspora traffic. ~2M pax.

name: Aeroportul Internațional Sibiu
notes: Sibiu County Council majority; Romanian state minority. Serves central Transylvania. Growing low-cost carrier traffic. ~500K pax.

name: Aeroportul Internațional Suceava Ștefan cel Mare
notes: Suceava County Council majority. Serves Bukovina and northeast Romania. Growing Wizz Air service. ~500K pax.

name: Aeroportul Transilvania Târgu Mureș
notes: Mureș County Council majority. Serves central Transylvania. Wizz Air base. ~400K pax.

name: Aeroportul Internațional George Enescu Bacău
notes: Bacău County Council majority. Serves western Moldova region. Strong diaspora traffic to Italy and UK. ~700K pax.

name: Aeroportul Internațional Craiova
notes: Dolj County Council majority; Romanian state minority. Serves Oltenia (southwest Romania). Growing Wizz Air traffic. ~400K pax.

name: Debrecen International Airport (Debreceni Nemzetközi Repülőtér)
notes: City of Debrecen and Hajdú-Bihar County. Hungary's second airport with scheduled service. Wizz Air base. Serves eastern Hungary. ~600K pax.

name: Aeroporto Guglielmo Marconi di Bologna SpA
notes: Bologna Municipality ~3.8%; Emilia-Romagna Region ~3.3%; Province of Bologna ~2.3%; Camera di Commercio Bologna ~11.5%; other public ~23.1%; free float ~56% on Borsa Italiana (ticker: ADB.MI). Italy's 7th-busiest airport. Ryanair's largest Italian base.

name: SAC Società Aeroporto Catania SpA
notes: Camera di Commercio di Catania ~61%; City of Catania ~12%; F2i SGR and 2i Aeroporti ~27%. Sicily's busiest airport and Italy's 6th-busiest. Gateway to Mount Etna and eastern Sicily. ~12M pax.

name: Gesap SpA (Aeroporto di Palermo Falcone e Borsellino)
notes: Provincia Regionale di Palermo ~39.1%; Camera di Commercio Palermo ~29.0%; other public entities ~6.5%; Airgest (Trapani) ~3.2%. Western Sicily's main airport. ~8M pax.

name: Geasar SpA (Aeroporto Olbia Costa Smeralda)
notes: Alisarda/Meridiana founder's family (Aga Khan's interests) historically controlled; now F2i SGR holds majority via Costasmeralda SpA. Gateway to Costa Smeralda and northeast Sardinia. ~3M pax, heavily seasonal.

name: SOGAER SpA (Aeroporto di Cagliari Elmas)
notes: Camera di Commercio di Cagliari ~94.35% (quasi-public); F2i SGR ~5.65%. Sardinia's busiest airport. Gateway to southern Sardinia. ~5M pax.

name: SAGAT SpA (Aeroporto di Torino Caselle)
notes: 2i Aeroporti SpA (F2i/Asterion) 55.87%; Città Metropolitana di Torino (ex-Provincia) 5%; Region of Piedmont 8%; Camera di Commercio di Torino ~5%; other public ~20%; private minority. Major Ryanair base. ~4.5M pax.

name: Aeroporto Friuli Venezia Giulia SpA (Trieste Airport)
notes: Autonomous Region Friuli Venezia Giulia ~82%; local public entities ~18%. Serves Trieste and northeast Italy/Slovenia border region. ~900K pax.

name: Aeroporto di Genova SpA
notes: Autorità di Sistema Portuale del Mar Ligure Occidentale (port authority) 60%; Camera di Commercio di Genova 25%; City of Genoa 15%. Italy's main Ligurian coast airport. ~1.5M pax.

name: SACAL SpA (Società Aeroportuale Calabrese)
notes: Calabria Region ~26%; Province of Catanzaro ~19%; local municipalities and public entities ~45%. Calabria's main airport. Also manages Reggio Calabria (REG) and Crotone (CRV). ~3M pax.

name: Aeroporti di Sardegna SpA (Alghero Airport)
notes: F2i SGR ~79.8%; Camera di Commercio di Sassari and local public entities ~20.2%. Northwest Sardinia's airport. Ryanair base. ~1.5M pax.

name: Aeroporto Valerio Catullo di Verona Villafranca SpA
notes: Save SpA (Venice) 70.98%; local public entities (Province of Verona, Camera di Commercio, Aerogest) ~29%. Managed by Save group. Serves Lake Garda and Verona. Also operates Brescia (VBS). ~4M pax.

name: Górnośląskie Towarzystwo Lotnicze SA (GTL)
notes: Silesian Voivodeship (Sejmik Śląski) ~72%; PPL (Polish state airports) ~28%. Poland's third-busiest airport. Wizz Air's largest Polish base. Serves the Upper Silesian industrial region. ~6M pax.

name: Port Lotniczy Łódź im. Władysława Reymonta Sp. z o.o.
notes: City of Łódź ~72%; Łódź Voivodeship ~28%. Poland's central airport. Limited scheduled service. ~300K pax.

name: Port Lotniczy Lublin SA
notes: Lublin Voivodeship ~64%; City of Lublin ~16%; surrounding municipalities ~20%. Eastern Poland's newest airport (opened 2012). Wizz Air and Ryanair service. ~600K pax.

name: Međunarodni Aerodrom Sarajevo d.o.o.
notes: State-owned enterprise under the Federation of Bosnia and Herzegovina. Bosnia's main international gateway. ~1.2M pax. EBRD and EU-funded modernization projects.

name: Aerodrom Tuzla d.o.o.
notes: Owned by Tuzla Canton. Bosnia's second busiest airport. Ryanair hub. ~800K pax.

name: Aerodrom Mostar d.o.o.
notes: State-owned, mainly seasonal charter flights. Serves Herzegovina tourism region. Small operations ~30K pax.

name: Aerodrom Banja Luka d.o.o.
notes: Owned by Republika Srpska. Ryanair and Air Serbia service. ~300K pax. Rapid growth since 2022.

name: United Airports of Georgia LLC
notes: State-owned company operating Kutaisi airport. Georgia's second airport, major Wizz Air base. ~1M pax. Separate from TAV-operated Tbilisi and Batumi.

name: Plovdiv Airport EAD
notes: State-owned via Bulgarian Ministry of Transport. Small operations, mainly seasonal charters. New terminal opened. Seeking private concession operator.

name: Kalaallit Airports A/S
notes: Greenland government 67%; Danish state investment fund 33%. Building new international airports at Nuuk and Ilulissat. Also operates Kangerlussuaq (former US air base).

name: Limak Kosovo International Airport J.S.C.
notes: Turkish-French consortium (Limak Holding + Aéroports de Lyon). 20-year DBFOT concession since 2011. Kosovo's only international airport. ~2.3M pax.

name: Zračna Luka Brač d.o.o.
notes: State-owned, operates Croatia's youngest commercial airport on the island of Brač. Seasonal tourism traffic. Terminal opened 2007.

name: Zračna Luka Mali Lošinj d.o.o.
notes: Privately owned public airport on Lošinj island. Small seasonal operations, domestic and international traffic.

name: Letiště Karlovy Vary s.r.o.
notes: 100% owned by Karlovy Vary Region. Czech Republic's fourth-busiest airport. Spa tourism and charter traffic.

name: East Bohemian Airport a.s.
notes: Jointly owned by City of Pardubice and Pardubice Region. Civil-military airport, seasonal charter flights. Modernized terminal.

name: Întreprinderea de Stat Aeroportul Internațional Chișinău
notes: State-owned enterprise under the Republic of Moldova. Renationalized at the end of 2023 after the Chișinău Court of Appeal annulled the 2013 Russian-held concession (Avia Invest). IATA code changed from KIV to RMO on 2024-01-18.

name: Letisko Poprad-Tatry, a.s.
notes: Slovak Republic (Ministry of Transport) 97.61%; City of Poprad 1.67%; Town of Vysoké Tatry 0.72%. Joint-stock company formed 2005-01-01. One of the highest-elevation commercial airports in Central Europe (718m). Serves the High Tatras ski region.
